title: Scorecard rows 03/07 drop their chip + count to an orphan second line
problem: Rows with long principle names ('The universal destination of goods', 'Integral human development') overflow the body track, so .sc-body's flex-wrap:wrap (line 264) dumps the verdict chip and finding count onto a stranded, right-aligned second line while the other five rows keep them inline. Two of seven rows are twice the height of the others for no semantic reason, breaking the table rhythm and the row-to-row chip alignment the code's own comment (line 263) promises. Confirmed in before-scorecard-top-dark.png (row 03) in both themes and before-scorecard-mid-dark.png (row 07).
recommendation: Forbid the row from wrapping on desktop and let the principle NAME wrap internally instead — it already has min-width:0 and text-wrap:balance (line 138), so a long name becomes two balanced small-caps lines while §-range, chip and count stay on the first baseline. Trim the over-generous count column, and re-enable wrap only inside the existing 680px mobile query.
evidence: before-scorecard-top-dark.png / before-scorecard-top-light.png (row 03 wrapped, rows 01–02 inline), before-scorecard-mid-dark.png (row 07); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 263–269, 138
amendments: Land together with C36 so the mobile scorecard rules are written once, not twice.
reasons: taste: Y — Two of seven rows breaking the table rhythm is visibly wrong in the report's primary navigation, and the fix restores rather than adds; coordinate the 680px wrap re-enable with C36's flex rewrite of .sc-row.

risk: Y — Pure CSS, scoped to .sc-* selectors; name wraps internally via existing min-width:0 + balance so nowrap cannot overflow the 588px body track, and mobile wrap is explicitly restored in the existing 680px block.

value: Y — Verified in before-scorecard-top-dark.png: rows 03/07 are double-height with chip+count stranded on a second line while siblings stay inline — the primary nav table looks broken on first scroll.

title: Ratings key wraps mid-definition with a widowed 'it.' and a stranded '·' at line start
problem: The legend that teaches the whole rating system breaks as '…In tension — strains / it. · Violates — contradicts it.' — a two-word definition split across lines and a separator dot opening line 2 (confirmed in before-scorecard-top-dark.png and before-synthesis-dark.png). Root cause: ratingsKey() (lines 714–724) emits terms, definitions and '·' separators as one flat text run with no whitespace around the separator spans (their .5rem padding is not a break opportunity), and .ratings-key carries a one-off max-width:60ch (line 224) that guarantees the wrap lands mid-phrase.
recommendation: Wrap each 'Word — definition.' triple in a white-space:nowrap .rk-item span and put real spaces around each '·' so lines can only break at separators (longest item is ~24ch). Drop the 60ch override so the key inherits the shared measure (see C05), and reduce the separator padding to compensate for the real spaces.
evidence: before-scorecard-top-dark.png (lines 1–2 of the legend), before-scorecard-top-light.png, before-synthesis-dark.png (bottom); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 223–228, 714–724
reasons: taste: Y — A legend that teaches the rating system must not widow its own two-word definition; nowrap items plus real break opportunities is exactly the invisible polish this page is about (coordinated, not conflicting, with C05's removal of the 60ch override).
risk: Y — Longest nowrap item (~24ch) fits any plausible measure, JS stays textContent-only, and dropping the 60ch override safely defers to the content-col cap; coordinates cleanly with C05 and C13.

value: Y — Verified: the legend that teaches the entire rating system reads 'strains / it. · Violates' — the first explanatory text of the scorecard looks typeset by accident; coordinate with C05/C13 which also touch .ratings-key.

title: Code-caption file paths break mid-extension ('…hydrator.r / s:31,37')
problem: The figcaption location is injected as one unbroken text node (line 953) and .loc applies overflow-wrap:anywhere + word-break:break-word (line 333), so paths split at arbitrary characters — confirmed in before-boundary-dark.png where '…user_inferred_gender_query_hydrator.r' wraps before 's:31,37', cutting the .rs extension in half. For a developer audience a filename broken inside its extension reads as a typo. The masthead repo URL already solves this with <wbr> after each '/' (lines 692–698); the caption never got the same treatment.
recommendation: Build .loc the same way as the repo URL: insert <wbr> after each '/' so wraps land at path-segment boundaries, and drop word-break:break-word, keeping overflow-wrap:anywhere only as a last-resort guard. The longest real segment fits comfortably on its own line at .7rem mono.
evidence: before-boundary-dark.png (caption 'home-mixer/query_hydrators/user_inferred_gender_query_hydrator.r' / 's:31,37'); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 333, 953, 692–698
reasons: taste: Y — A filename broken inside its extension reads as a typo to the developer audience, and the fix reuses the file's own <wbr> pattern — pure coherence, zero new vocabulary.
risk: Y — Copies the proven masthead <wbr> pattern (lines 692–698) and keeps overflow-wrap:anywhere as last resort, so no new overflow path is introduced.
value: Y — A file path breaking inside its .rs extension reads as a typo to the exact developer audience this report must convince, and the <wbr> pattern already exists in the masthead.

title: Code figure's right-edge fade is always on — it washes out real terminal glyphs and never releases at scroll-end
problem: The 2rem gradient (.pre-wrap::after, lines 336–340) renders unconditionally: snippets whose last line genuinely ends inside the fade zone have their closing characters (');' in both theme shots) visibly half-erased, and when a reader scrolls an overflowing line to its end the fade persists, falsely signalling more content. On mobile, where the scrollbar is invisible, the fade is the only affordance and it lies at scroll-end on all 8 snippets. Compounding it, padding-right on a horizontal scroll container is not reliably part of scrollable overflow, so at scroll-end the last glyph abuts the 1px border with zero inset.
recommendation: Toggle the fade from the pre's scroll state with one passive listener per figure — visible only while content remains to the right — and move the right padding onto an inline-block code element so the final character always lands with the same .9rem inset the left edge gets. The reduced-motion block (line 410) already neutralizes the transition.
evidence: before-code-citations-dark.png and before-finding-card-light.png (terminal ');' dimmed; first line cut at 'weights.fa'); /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-code-end.png (fade persists at scrollLeft=max); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 336–344
amendments: Must land with C28 so print resets the new display/padding on code.

Use figure.code code{display:block; width:max-content; padding-right:.9rem} instead of inline-block (avoids the baseline descender gap inside pre), re-run syncFade on document.fonts.ready (CDN Fira Code load changes scrollWidth after the rAF), and land C28's print reset (display:block; padding-right:0) in the same change.
Land together with C28, which must reset the new display:inline-block/padding-right on code for print.
reasons: taste: Y — A decorative fade that half-erases real evidence glyphs and lies at scroll-end is Tufte's cardinal sin — decoration falsifying data; the scroll-state toggle is felt, never noticed; coordinate the inline-block code change with C28's print reset.

risk: Y — The fade-state JS is sound (passive listeners, no data paths), but the inline-block padding trick and font-load timing need the safer variant to avoid a visible vertical gap and a stale fade before fonts arrive.
value: Y — The report's own evidence glyphs are half-erased by an unconditional fade and the scroll affordance lies at scroll-end — an evidence-obscuring defect in an audit product.

title: The 'ONE prose measure' resolves to four+ different widths — footer rule 22% short, summary rule 18px long, code figure ~80px wide
problem: --prose is 52ch (line 59, documented as 'the ONE prose measure'), but ch resolves against each element's own font-size, and three blocks add explicit overrides. Measured: masthead/scorecard/findings rules = 474px; the summary's closing rule = 492.5px (.summary .content-col{max-width:54ch}, line 235, also catches the rule's wrapper); the footer rule = 367.5px — 107px short — because footer.report{font-size:.92rem} (line 397) shrinks the ch before the column cap computes, which also forces the credit to wrap mid-link ('Magnificent Humanity / Audit agent skill', before-footer-dark.png). Inside one card, .f-explain (1rem), remedy (1.03), quote (1.08) and figure.code (19px inherited) each cap at different widths, so the right edge wanders within a single finding. The dek (54ch, line 201) drifts too.
recommendation: Define the measure in a font-size-independent unit: --prose:29.6rem (exactly the 474px that 52ch resolves to at the 19px body). Every existing max-width:var(--prose) declaration then lands on one identical right edge — the footer is fixed automatically with no other change. Remove the three explicit overrides (dek 54ch, summary 54ch, ratings-key 60ch — the last as part of C02) so they inherit the unified measure.
css_js_sketch:
:root{--prose:29.6rem}  /* line 59 — 474px, i.e. 52ch at the 19px body, now font-size-independent */
.dek{max-width:none}                       /* line 201 */
.summary .content-col{max-width:var(--prose)}  /* line 235 */
/* .ratings-key 60ch removed in C02; footer needs no change — 29.6rem no longer shrinks at .92rem */
evidence: before-footer-dark.png / before-footer-light.png (short rule + mid-link wrap), before-scorecard-top-dark.png (summary rule), before-finding-card-dark.png (code figure wider than prose); measured 474 / 492.5 / 367.5px; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 59, 125, 201, 235, 397–398
amendments: Comment the 29.6rem with its derivation (52ch at the 19px body) so the magic number stays legible to future editors.
Verify the 29.6rem value against the rendered 52ch before hardcoding; the ratings-key override removal is already owned by C02.
reasons: taste: Y — The design's own promise is ONE prose measure and the footer rule visibly breaks it; converting --prose to a font-size-independent unit makes the page more itself with no visual change elsewhere.

risk: Y — 29.6rem is verified equal to the current 52ch at the 19px body, every var(--prose) consumer is a max-width so the unit swap cannot overflow --measure, and it removes the ch-resolves-per-font-size footgun for good.
value: Y — The footer rule visibly 22% short and the wandering right edge are real, and the single-token fix repairs them all at once.

title: Theme toggle is unanchored at every breakpoint: orphaned at wide widths, overlapping content at 681–940px, flush against the screen edge on mobile
problem: Three provable failures of one element. (1) At 1100px+ the fixed toggle (top/right 1.1rem, line 180) floats ~140px beyond the report's right text edge, an off-grid inset that matches no token (layout padding is 1.5rem, line 116). (2) The static fallback only kicks in at ≤680px (line 434), but the layout needs ~872px, so between 681 and ~940px (iPad portrait included) the opaque z-index:15 button sits ON the content track — measured at 760px it covers the scorecard's right-aligned '3 findings' count. (3) At ≤680px the static button is a direct child of <body> outside .layout's padding: measured at 375px it renders at x=0, border clipped by the screen edge, the first thing a mobile reader sees. Its tap target is also ~61x28px, well under touch minimums.
recommendation: Pin the toggle's right edge to the layout's right padding edge so it tracks the measure at any wide viewport; make it position:absolute below 940px so it sits in the masthead band and scrolls away instead of floating over data; give the ≤680px static fallback the layout's 1.5rem left margin; and bump padding toward a usable tap target. One element, on the same grid music as everything else.
evidence: before-masthead-dark.png / before-masthead-light.png (toggle ~140px past content edge); /tmp/mh-mobile/mid-760-scorecard.png (button over '3 findings'); /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-pagetop.png (button at x=0, border clipped); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 60, 116, 179–184, 414, 433–434
reasons: taste: Y — A control that floats off-grid at wide widths, covers data at tablet widths, and clips at the screen edge on mobile is embarrassing in a release; pinning it to the layout's grid is restraint, not chrome.

risk: Y — The right:max(...) calc correctly tracks the layout's padding edge, the 940px absolute block is ordered before the existing 680px static rule so the cascade resolves correctly, and the measured 681–940px content overlap is a real release-blocker; only residual nit is ~7px scrollbar skew in 100vw, acceptable.

value: Y — Verified failures at three breakpoints including the toggle overlapping scorecard data at tablet widths and rendering clipped at x=0 as a mobile reader's first impression — functional breakage, not polish.

title: Dark theme's muted-text tier is collapsed: --tx-2 should be base-500, not base-300
problem: Light has a real three-step hierarchy (body 18.6:1 vs secondary base-600 at 5.0:1 — a 3.7x ratio). Dark sets --tx-2 to base-300 (line 68), only a 1.29x step from body base-200 (9.3:1 vs 12.0:1), so the dek, meta labels, section captions, glosses and footer all read at near-body weight — the default theme looks flat where light looks layered (compare before-masthead-dark.png vs before-masthead-light.png). This also deviates from the Flexoki spec itself, which assigns dark tx-2 = base-500. Separately, --tx-3 is defined in both themes (lines 35, 68) but consumed nowhere — a dead token (grep confirms).
recommendation: Set dark --tx-2 to base-500 per the Flexoki spec: 5.19:1 on black still clears the file's own 4.5:1 floor at the smallest tx-2 sizes (.64–.7rem mono labels) and lands almost exactly on light's 4.97:1 — true parity. Either delete the unused --tx-3 or put it to work on the faintest marks (e.g. the dropped-list dash bullets).
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 35, 68; grep: --tx-3 declared twice, used zero times; before-masthead-dark.png vs before-masthead-light.png; computed base-300/#100F0F = 9.31:1 vs base-200 = 11.98:1
amendments: Just delete the dead --tx-3 token rather than inventing a new use for it; add uses only when a real need appears.

If C23 also lands, add a dark override for figure.code figcaption (e.g. color:var(--base-300)) — base-500 on base-900 measures 4.04:1, under the file's 4.5 floor; base-500 is fine on black (5.19) and base-950 (4.67).
reasons: taste: Y — The default theme reading flat where light reads layered is a hierarchy failure, and base-500 is what the Flexoki spec itself prescribes — this is conforming to the settled palette, not changing it.
risk: Y — Matches the Flexoki spec and verifies AA on every current dark surface, but CONFLICTS with C23 on the code figcaption — the amendment resolves it.

value: Y — Dark is the default theme and its collapsed text hierarchy makes every page harder to scan than light; restoring the Flexoki-spec base-500 gives the reader back the layering the design promises.

title: Finding IDs are displayed nowhere, leaving 'reason merged into F1' a dangling, unlinked cross-reference
problem: Findings carry stable ids (anchors #f-F1…, line 902) but the ID is never rendered — the rail shows only star/engages/confidence (lines 904–928). Yet the 'Dropped and consolidated' closing prints 'reason merged into F1' as plain text (fmtReason, lines 1025–1028; visible in before-footer-dark.png), a reference to a label that does not exist anywhere on the page and is not a link. A reader cannot resolve which finding 'F1' is, and the flat list of near-identical cards gives no citable identity or positional sense.
recommendation: Render the finding's ID as a small Fira Code label beside the rail ✴ — same quiet mono voice as the confidence number — so every card has a visible, citable identity. Then make 'merged into F1' a real anchor to #f-F1 (only when the target exists). Pairs with C24's permalink/:target work.
evidence: before-footer-dark.png / before-closings-dark.png ('reason merged / into F1'); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 902, 904–928, 1025–1028, 1111–1112
amendments: Coordinate with C24 — the railhead wrapper and the star-as-link must be built in one pass.
Coordinate the rail-head markup with C24's permalink star so the two changes share one structure.
reasons: taste: Y — 'Merged into F1' referencing a label that exists nowhere is a broken promise to the reader; a quiet mono ID in the rail is information, not decoration, for an artifact meant to be cited.

risk: Y — Renders via el()/textContent with a getElementById existence guard, findings render before the dropped list so the anchor check is sound; coordinate with C24 (star becomes an <a>) and C38 (both rewrite the dropped reason nodes) in one pass.

value: Y — Verified: 'reason merged into F1' references a label rendered nowhere — an unresolvable cross-reference in a rigor-themed audit is a direct trust break, and visible IDs make findings citable in issues.

title: Printing from the default dark theme is broken — only 4 of ~30 tokens reset, code panels print near-black with #111 text
problem: @media print (lines 439–452) overrides only --bg/--bg-2/--tx/--tx-2. Because dark is the default, [data-theme="dark"] still supplies everything else at print time: --code-bg stays base-950 so code panels print as near-black slabs holding forced #111 text (illegible); accents and verdict colors stay pale 400-level on white; chip tints stay color-mix'd with --black (dark pills on paper); --ui-3 hairlines (base-800) vanish. Print/PDF is a first-class path for a report artifact. The few values that ARE reset are also non-Flexoki grays (#111/#444 line 440, #666 line 450) — the only colors in the file that bypass the ramp.
recommendation: Inside @media print, re-declare the full LIGHT semantic token set under ':root,[data-theme="dark"]' (equal specificity, later in cascade — wins), using Flexoki inks instead of the generic grays: --tx:var(--black), --tx-2:var(--base-700), URL suffix var(--base-600). ~15 lines of pure CSS, no markup change.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 439–452 (print block) vs 66–88 (dark tokens that survive); line 71 --code-bg:base-950 + line 331 figure.code uses it with color:var(--tx)
amendments: Also reset --rk-honors-ink/--rk-tension-ink/--rk-violates-ink to the light values (sketch omits them, leaving pale 400-level key words on white in print), and consider the light color-mix tints instead of #fff so chips keep their wash.
reasons: taste: Y — Print/PDF is a first-class path for a report artifact and it currently ships near-black code slabs from the default theme; re-declaring the light tokens in Flexoki inks also purges the only non-ramp grays in the file.
risk: Y — Pure CSS at equal specificity later in cascade, all referenced ramp vars exist at :root, and it fixes a genuinely broken default-theme print path.
value: Y — Verified the print block resets only 4 of ~30 tokens while dark is default — print/PDF is how a CTO circulates this report, and near-black code slabs with #111 text are illegible.

title: UA chrome ignores the active theme: bind the color-scheme property and a theme-color meta to data-theme
problem: The only color-scheme declaration is the meta on line 6 ('light dark'), which resolves UA surfaces from the OS preference — but the page forces dark via data-theme regardless of OS. A light-OS machine (Windows, Linux, macOS with always-visible scrollbars) renders the default dark page with bright light root scrollbars, plus light UA scrollbars inside every one of the 8 overflowing code panes; a dark-OS user gets the inverse on the light theme. No CSS color-scheme property exists in either token block (grep confirms), and there is no theme-color meta, so mobile browser chrome stays white above the #100F0F page.
recommendation: Declare color-scheme on the theme roots so all UA-rendered surfaces follow the toggle, add a theme-color meta, and sync it in wireTheme()'s set() alongside localStorage.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html line 6 (meta only); grep: no color-scheme property in lines 33–88, no theme-color meta; lines 455–466, 1055–1060 (theme bootstrap/toggle)
reasons: taste: Y — Light UA scrollbars on the default dark page (and white mobile chrome over #100F0F) is visibly wrong machinery the reader never asked to see; color-scheme plus theme-color is invisible correctness.
risk: Y — Two declarations plus a meta sync inside the existing set(); the pre-paint script already sets data-theme before first paint so color-scheme resolves correctly with no flash.
value: Y — Light UA scrollbars on the default dark page (root plus all 8 code panes) and white mobile chrome are visible on common configurations, and the fix is three declarations.

title: Print clips long code lines: the code child's white-space:pre defeats the print pre-wrap rule
problem: The print block sets figure.code pre{white-space:pre-wrap; overflow:visible} (line 451), but line 344 sets figure.code code{white-space:pre}, and that specified value on the code element beats the inherited pre-wrap at print time. With figure.code{overflow:hidden} (line 331) still in force, overflowing lines are silently truncated at the figure edge in every printed/PDF'd report — lost evidence in a report artifact.
recommendation: Extend the print rule to the code child so the wrap actually applies. One added selector. (If C04's display:inline-block lands on code, also reset it to display:block here.)
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 331, 344 vs 451; the overflow itself visible in before-code-citations-dark.png
amendments: Ship in the same change as C04 and C09 as one coherent print/code fix.
reasons: taste: Y — Silently truncated evidence in every printed report is data loss in an audit artifact; once C09 makes print first-class this one-selector fix is non-negotiable, and it already anticipates C04's inline-block change.

risk: Y — The white-space:pre on code provably defeats the print pre-wrap rule (line 344 vs 451), silently truncating evidence in every PDF; one added selector, and it already anticipates C04's display change.
value: Y — Silently truncated evidence lines in every printed copy means lost proof in the artifact's most shareable form.

title: Polish the hero verdict pill: baseline-align the OVERALL eyebrow, let it recede, and bind the pill to its caption
problem: Three small flaws compound on the page's single most important verdict. (1) .verdict-image uses align-items:center (line 165), so the .66rem mono eyebrow's baseline floats ~2px above the 1.02rem small-caps value — visible at reading size. (2) The eyebrow is explicitly opacity:1 (line 174) in the same color as the value with only a .55rem gap, so the pill mushes into one phrase, 'OVERALL TOWARD BABEL', label competing with verdict. (3) Spacing above the pill (1rem, line 233) and below it (1.2rem, line 238) are near-equal, so it floats ambiguously between the 'Synthesis' caption and the prose instead of reading as the section's headline. Keep the outline-only treatment — the 'distinct tier' styling is deliberate (comment, lines 162–163).
recommendation: Switch the pill to baseline alignment, let the eyebrow recede via opacity alone (no new borders or fills), and retune the caption/pill/prose margins so caption + verdict read as one head unit followed by the argument.
evidence: before-synthesis-dark.png (3x zoom: OVERALL baseline high; near-equal gaps above/below pill); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 164–175, 231–238
amendments: Replace .vi-eyebrow{opacity:.72} with color:var(--tx-2) (or keep opacity:1): the composited .72 eyebrow measures 3.55:1 on dark violates — an AA failure at .66rem; baseline alignment and the margin retune are safe as sketched.
reasons: taste: Y — Baseline alignment, a receding eyebrow, and asymmetric spacing around the page's single most important verdict are exactly the polish a careful reader feels but never notices.
risk: Y — Two of three fixes are safe pure CSS, but the opacity recede provably breaks the dark-theme AA floor and needs the token-based variant.

value: Y — The eyebrow mushing into 'OVERALL TOWARD BABEL' mildly hurts the page's key verdict, but the baseline and margin tweaks are designer-grade; cheap enough to ride along — coordinate margins with C47.

title: Move the ratings key below 'The findings, by principle' caption
problem: renderScorecard() appends ratingsKey() BEFORE the h2 caption (lines 763–765), so the section opens with an unanchored legend sentence floating between the synthesis-closing rule and the heading it belongs to (before-scorecard-top-dark.png). Structurally, content precedes the very heading the section is aria-labelledby'd to (line 477) — poor document outline. A legend belongs under its section title, directly above the chips it explains.
recommendation: Swap the append order — caption first, then key — and retune margins so the key reads as the section's legend (slightly smaller, tighter to the first row) rather than stray body prose. Combine with C02's nowrap items.
evidence: before-scorecard-top-dark.png, before-synthesis-dark.png (legend above the caption); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 477, 759–766
reasons: taste: Y — A legend floating above the heading it belongs to reads as a mistake and inverts the document outline; caption-then-key is the obvious quiet order (coordinate margins with C02).
risk: Y — A two-line append-order swap that also fixes the aria-labelledby outline (content currently precedes its own labelling h2); margins coordinate with C02 without overlap.

value: Y — Verified: the legend floats above the heading it belongs to, reading as orphaned synthesis text — moving it under the caption fixes comprehension and document outline together; sequence with C02.

title: Scorecard finding rows — the report's primary navigation — have no resting link affordance and snap on hover
problem: The global reset strips underlines (line 99) and the rows render in plain body ink; nothing at rest marks them as links except the small accent ✴, while the masthead and footer links are accent + underline (lines 214, 400) — two link grammars on one page, with the primary nav the unmarked one. On hover the only feedback is an instant color snap (lines 276–278) with no transition.
recommendation: Keep the quiet list aesthetic but give the title a hairline resting underline using the existing --ui-3 token, resolving to accent on hover/focus with a .15s ease — the same underline grammar as the masthead/footer links, dialed down.
css_js_sketch:
.scf-title{text-decoration:underline; text-decoration-thickness:1px; text-decoration-color:var(--ui-3); text-underline-offset:3px; transition:color .15s ease, text-decoration-color .15s ease}
.sc-findings a:hover .scf-title,.sc-findings a:focus-visible .scf-title{color:var(--accent); text-decoration-color:currentColor}
evidence: before-scorecard-top-dark.png / before-scorecard-top-light.png (rows read as static list items); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 99, 214, 275–280, 400
reasons: taste: Y — Two link grammars on one page with the primary navigation unmarked is incoherent; a hairline --ui-3 resting underline resolving to accent is the existing grammar dialed down, not new decoration.
risk: Y — Decorative underline uses the existing --ui-3 token (no text-contrast requirement), and the transition is already neutralized by the reduced-motion block at line 410.
value: Y — The report's primary navigation is the one set of links with no resting affordance — readers who don't discover the rows are clickable lose the whole jump-to-finding workflow.

title: Pin the scorecard relation marker to the right rail — its column alignment is an accident of this dataset
problem: Only .scf-conf carries margin-left:auto (line 284); .scf-rel (line 282) sits flush after the title. The clean right-hand IN TENSION/VIOLATES + confidence column in the screenshots exists only because every title in this dataset happens to wrap to two lines at 1100px, forcing the title box to full width. Any one-line title (shorter finding, wider viewport, different audit) strands its relation label mid-line and shatters the column. A flagship template cannot depend on titles being long.
recommendation: Move the auto margin from the confidence number to the relation marker so the rel+conf pair is always pushed to the right edge regardless of title length; keep the existing fixed gap between them.
css_js_sketch:
.scf-rel{margin-left:auto; padding-left:.9rem}
.scf-conf{margin-left:0}
evidence: /tmp/sc-crops/relconf-col-dark.png (column aligns, but every title is 2 lines); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 275, 282–284
reasons: taste: Y — A flagship template cannot let its right-hand relation column depend on every title happening to wrap; moving margin-left:auto to the rel marker makes the design intentional instead of accidental.
risk: Y — One-property flex change that removes a dataset-dependent alignment accident; title min-width:0 already guarantees shrink behavior with one-line titles.
value: Y — This is a template product, and the right-column alignment surviving only because every current title happens to wrap two lines is a latent break for the very next audit.

title: Scorecard's closing rule collides with its own full-measure hairlines
problem: The scorecard's internal group hairlines span the full grid (measured 162→937px) — correct, since the rail holds row numbers and chips right-align to that edge. But the rule that closes the section is nested in a .content-col (lines 852–854), so it runs 350→823px: starting 188px right of the hairlines above it and stopping 114px short of their right edge. At the scorecard/findings boundary the short rule reads as a glitch where the table system and the prose system collide.
recommendation: Let the one rule that closes the scorecard span the same full measure as the table's own hairlines — append it directly to the section instead of nesting it in the rail grid. Prose sections keep their content-edge rules; the table announces its own end on its own grid.
css_js_sketch:
/* renderScorecard, replacing lines 852–854 */
sec.appendChild(el("hr","rule block-rule"));  // spans rail+gutter+content like .sc-group borders
evidence: before-scorecard-mid-dark.png (hairlines x=162–937 vs closing rule x=350–823, y≈1064); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 250, 852–854
reasons: taste: Y — The short rule colliding with the table's own full-grid hairlines reads as a glitch; letting the table close on its own grid is the coherent resolution of the two systems.
risk: Y — Appending the hr to the section instead of the rail grid is a two-line JS change; print's .grid display:block doesn't touch a direct section child, and mobile renders it identically.
value: Y — A closing rule starting 188px right of the hairlines directly above it reads as a rendering glitch at the most-trafficked section boundary.

title: 'confidence' and 'ENGAGES' speak in different voices 1.5rem apart in the same rail
problem: The same Fira Code labelling voice is set five ways: .fe-label is uppercase with .1em tracking (lines 307–310) while .f-conf directly beneath it in the same rail stack is lowercase with no tracking (line 314); masthead labels are lowercase .08em (line 210), the pill eyebrow and toggle uppercase .12em (lines 174, 182), and .dc-label .04em (line 393). The rail clash ('ENGAGES … confidence 88') is unmistakable once seen, on every card in both themes. The stylesheet defines tracking tokens for small-caps (lines 29–30) but none for the mono voice.
recommendation: Add a --track-mono token (.1em) and give .f-conf the .fe-label treatment (uppercase, .68rem, .1em) so the rail speaks with one voice — pure CSS, since the bold number re-declares its own size/tracking (line 315) and digits are unaffected by text-transform. Align .dc-label and .mm-label to the same token (the lowercase-keys vs uppercase-headings case split is fine if consistent).
evidence: before-finding-card-dark.png / before-finding-card-light.png ('ENGAGES' tracked vs 'confidence' untracked); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 174, 182, 209–212, 307–315, 392–393
reasons: taste: Y — 'ENGAGES' and 'confidence' speaking in different voices 1.5rem apart in the same rail is unmistakable once seen; one --track-mono token is exactly how this stylesheet already thinks.
risk: Y — Pure CSS; the bold value re-declares size/tracking at line 315 and digits are immune to text-transform, so only the label's voice changes as intended.
value: Y — Verified: 'ENGAGES' and 'confidence' speak in two voices 1.5rem apart on every single card, and the fix is pure CSS with a reusable token.

title: The em-dash in the rail ENGAGES list wraps inconsistently — bind it to the relation word
problem: The dash lives in the breakable principle text node ('shortName + " — "', lines 916–917) while only the relation span is nowrap (line 312), so line breaks fall on either side of it. Both failure modes appear in the shipped screenshots: 'Integral development / — violates' (dash opens line 2) and 'Social justice — / violates' (dash dangles at line end, before-boundary-dark.png). One list, two wrap grammars.
recommendation: Move the dash into the no-wrap relation group so a wrap always reads 'Principle / — relation', never 'Principle — / relation'. Optionally add a small hanging indent so wrapped relation lines read as subordinate.
evidence: before-finding-card-dark.png, before-finding-card-light.png, before-boundary-dark.png (both wrap patterns); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 311–313, 916–917
reasons: taste: Y — Two wrap grammars in one list — dash opening one line, dangling at another — is micro-typographic noise; binding the dash to the relation word makes every wrap read identically.

risk: Y — Safe textContent-only restructure, but scope the optional .65rem hanging indent to desktop — it conflicts with C58's mobile display:contents row where the negative text-indent reads as a stray left shift.
value: Y — Both wrap failures ('— violates' opening a line, dash dangling) are visible in shipped screenshots on the element repeated beside every finding.

title: Collapse the eight near-identical body sizes (and the remedy > explanation inversion) to a three-step scale
problem: Running text is set at eight nearly-identical sizes with five line-heights: f-explain 1.0/1.58 (line 327), remedy li 1.03/1.5 (371), plain li 1.03/1.55 (383), cite-gloss .96/1.45 (362), structural .98/1.5 (376), sc-note .98/1.45 (287), lead 1.0/1.5 (381), summary 1.06/1.56 (236), footer .92/1.55 (397). Within one card the remedy bullets are 3% larger and looser than the explanation above them — an inverted hierarchy (the card's argument is its smallest text), perceptible as 'something is off' without reading as intent. Paragraph gaps also drift (.8rem in f-explain, line 328, vs .85rem in content-col, line 126).
recommendation: Tokenize a three-step prose scale and map every class onto it: primary 1rem/1.55 (f-explain, remedy, plain, lead), secondary .96rem/1.5 (gloss, structural, sc-note), small .92rem/1.55 (footer, dropped). Keep summary at 1.06/1.55 as the deliberate lead tier. Unify the paragraph gap on .85rem.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 126, 236, 287, 327–328, 362, 371, 376, 381, 383, 397; before-finding-card-dark.png (remedy optically heavier than explanation)
amendments: Keep the summary's 1.06 lead tier as stated, and land alongside C55 so the prose and mono scales are tokenized in one pass.
Add the missing ul.plain.dropped li{font-size:.92rem} the prose promises but the sketch omits, and land after C05 so size changes can't move ch-based measures.
reasons: taste: Y — Eight near-identical sizes with the card's argument set smaller than its bullets reads as drift, not hierarchy; a three-step tokenized scale is the systemic restraint this page deserves.
risk: Y — Token mapping is low risk and the remedy>explanation inversion is real, but the sketch under-specifies the dropped tier.
value: Y — The remedy-larger-than-explanation inversion is the kind of 'something is off' a reader feels without naming, and collapsing eight sizes to three tokens prevents future drift.

title: Citation quote marks: make them real characters and hang the opening mark outside the text edge
problem: Two related defects on the report's most precious typography. (1) The CSS-supplied opening “ (.cite-quote::before, line 361) sits inside the text column, pushing the quote's first letter ~0.5em right of the shared left edge while the gloss below sits flush — a visible left-edge wobble on every citation pair, in a design built on ONE shared left edge. (2) Because both marks are pseudo-content, selecting/copying a quote yields it WITHOUT its quotation marks — poor copy fidelity for the one element built to be quoted, and invisible to find-in-page.
recommendation: Emit the curly marks as real text in JS (q.textContent = '“'+curlQuote(...)+'”'), delete the pseudo-elements, and hang the opening mark with a small negative text-indent tuned for EB Garamond italic, with hanging-punctuation as progressive enhancement. One change fixes alignment, copy, and search at once.
evidence: before-code-citations-dark.png / before-finding-card-light.png (quote first letters indented vs flush gloss; §104 line 1 at ≈418px vs line 2 at the 400px edge at 2x); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 356–362, 980
reasons: taste: Y — Real quotation marks fix copy fidelity, find-in-page, and the left-edge wobble at once — hanging the opening mark is classical typography in service of the page's one-left-edge creed.

risk: Y — The -.5em hang (≈8.6px) fits inside the .8rem ref gap so it cannot collide with the §-column, curlQuote already normalizes inner doubles so the real outer marks are safe, and the 680px reset is included; copy/search fidelity is a genuine win.
value: Y — Copy fidelity and find-in-page on the report's most quotable element are real reader value, and the fix simultaneously repairs the visible left-edge wobble against the gloss.

title: Remedy text and the structural note sit on their own indented left edges — hang their marks in the gutter instead
problem: The page's core rule is one shared left text edge with marks hanging left of it (the §-refs do exactly this). But remedy items use padding-left:1.6rem with the ✦ at left:0 (lines 371–372), pushing remedy text 1.6rem right of the edge; and .structural puts its 2px rule AT the edge with 1rem padding (line 375), creating a third edge. Measured in the light crop at 2x: content edge ≈400px, structural text ≈435px, remedy text ≈452px — the finding tail accumulates three competing left edges.
recommendation: Treat the ✦ and the structural rule like the §-ref: hang them in the gutter and return the text to the shared content edge. Restore the indents only at ≤680px where the gutter disappears (mirroring the existing .cite fallback).
evidence: before-finding-card-light.png (three left edges: 400 / 435 / 452px at 2x), before-code-citations-dark.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 370–376
amendments: Mirror the ≤680px resets in @media print too — print keeps .layout's 1.5rem padding and the -1.6rem hang slightly exceeds it.
reasons: taste: Y — Three competing left edges in the finding tail contradict the page's core rule; hanging the ✦ and the structural rule in the gutter like the §-refs makes the page more itself, with the existing 680px fallback pattern.
risk: Y — The 2.25rem gutter comfortably absorbs both hangs on desktop and the mobile fallback is specified; only the print path needs the small guard.
value: Y — Indented remedy bullets read as normal to any reader — this strengthens the design's one-edge thesis but only a designer would ever flag it; acceptable as a bundled nicety with C22.

title: Remedies visually attach to the citation above them — give the block real separation
problem: Citations get a labeled movement ('From Magnifica Humanitas', line 350) but remedies start only 1.2rem after the last gloss (ul.remedy margin-top, line 370) — barely more than the 1rem between successive citations (line 357). In both theme shots the ✦ list reads as overflow of the last quote, so the report's most actionable content has the weakest sectional identity on the page.
recommendation: Widen the gap to ~2rem so the block reads as a new movement; optionally mirror the cite-cap with a quiet small-caps 'Remedies' cap in tx-2, bound tightly to its list (the cap is the fuller fix but the gap alone restores the grouping).
evidence: before-code-citations-dark.png, before-finding-card-light.png (gloss→remedy gap ≈ citation→citation gap); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 350, 357, 370, 987–992
amendments: Take the 2rem gap only; drop the optional 'Remedies' cap — the code's own comment says 'no label' and that restraint is the design.
Take the optional 'Remedies' cap, not just the gap — scannable action items are what a CTO acts on.
reasons: taste: Y — The report's most actionable content reading as overflow of the last quote is a real grouping failure, but the gap alone restores it.
risk: Y — The margin widening is one safe declaration; the optional 'Remedies' cap reuses the existing small-caps caption vocabulary and is gated on remediation.length.
value: Y — Verified: the most actionable content on the page reads as overflow of the last quote, weakening exactly the section a decision-maker needs to find.

title: Dark code panel is one ramp step off black and visually disappears — use base-900 for surface parity with light
problem: In light, --code-bg is base-100 — two steps off the paper (1.24:1 surface contrast) and the block clearly reads as a panel. In dark it is base-950 (line 71) — one step, 1.11:1 — so the panel surface vanishes and the block is held together by its border alone. Same component, different material presence per theme.
recommendation: Move dark --code-bg to base-900 (1.28:1 vs black — the same perceptual offset as light, and the same semantic slot: dark's --ui step). Nudge three syntax tokens to keep the file's documented 4.5:1 floor on the new surface, using the derive-off-the-ramp precedent already in the file (#5290C5, line 74). The pre-wrap fade keys off var(--code-bg) and follows automatically.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 38, 71–74, 331, 339; before-finding-card-light.png vs before-code-citations-dark.png; computed base-950 vs #100F0F = 1.11:1, base-900 = 1.28:1, light base-100 vs paper = 1.24:1
amendments: Also nudge --hl-function (orange-400 measures 4.498:1 on base-900 — exactly at the floor, e.g. lighten one step) and, if C07 lands, override the dark code figcaption color (base-500 on base-900 = 4.04:1 fails).
reasons: taste: Y — Same component, different material presence per theme is a parity failure; base-900 is the same semantic slot light uses, and the off-ramp contrast nudges follow the file's own #5290C5 precedent.
risk: Y — Surface parity is right and most tokens verify (cyan 5.22, green 4.77), but two contrast edges need the amendment; CONFLICTS with C07 on the figcaption.
value: Y — The dark code panel still reads fine via its border and syntax color in the screenshots — surface parity is a connoisseur point, but the contrast math is done and it's token-only.

title: Make the rail ✴ a permalink and style :target so jumps visibly land
problem: Findings have stable anchors and the scorecard deep-links to them (line 822), but the star is an inert aria-hidden span (line 906) and grep confirms no :target rule exists — a reader who wants to cite one finding has to open devtools, and after a smooth-scroll jump the landed card looks identical to every neighbor (with scroll-margin-top:5rem the previous finding's tail is visible above, so there is no confirmation of which card answered the click). For a report meant to be cited in issues and PRs, deep-linking is core product behavior.
recommendation: Render the ✴ as a self-link (<a href="#f-{id}">) with an aria-label — the finding's sigil becomes its permalink, zero new decoration — and add a quiet persistent :target state in the existing vocabulary (title takes accent while the hash matches), plus an optional one-shot wash already neutralized by the reduced-motion block (line 410). Clicking the star also puts the copyable URL in the address bar.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 90, 291, 822, 905–907, 935; grep ':target' returns nothing
amendments: Keep the persistent :target title accent; omit the one-shot background wash animation — the quiet state is confirmation enough.
Build the star-anchor alongside C08's rail-head ID so the rail is touched once.
reasons: taste: Y — For an artifact cited in issues and PRs, the sigil-as-permalink is function disguised as the existing glyph, and a quiet :target state confirms the jump; coordinate the star markup with C08.

risk: Y — The anchor keeps the .f-star class so existing rail styles apply, :target plus the motion-gated wash uses only existing tokens (accent verifies 4.83:1 dark / 6.39:1 light on the title), and it must merge with C08's railhead wrapper in one edit.
value: Y — Citing individual findings in issues and PRs is core product behavior for this artifact, and landing a jump with zero confirmation of which card answered is a real wayfinding failure.

title: Deep links land off-target: re-scroll to the URL fragment after render and after fonts load
problem: Content is built at DOMContentLoaded (lines 1124–1125), so when someone opens report.html#f-F4 the target does not exist at parse time; and even after the browser's fragment retry, EB Garamond arrives from the CDN and reflows the entire serif page — on a document this long the anchor shifts substantially, parking the reader mid-way through the wrong finding.
recommendation: After init() finishes, and again when document.fonts.ready resolves, re-scroll to location.hash if present. Three lines of vanilla JS, fully self-contained.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 1124–1125 (render at DOMContentLoaded), Google Fonts link line 11, line 90 (smooth scroll)
reasons: taste: Y — Deep links that park the reader mid-way through the wrong finding break the product behavior C24 establishes; three invisible lines of vanilla JS, fully in-budget.
risk: Y — Three lines, guarded on location.hash, self-contained; the only edge (a late fonts.ready re-scroll) targets the same fragment the reader navigated to, which is harmless.
value: Y — Deep links that park readers mid-wrong-finding after the Garamond reflow break the share moment entirely — and C24's permalinks are only as good as where they land.

title: The page dead-ends: add a quiet wayfinding line (back to top · findings index) above the footer credit
problem: This is one very long flat flow and there is NO route back up from anywhere: grep finds no '#top', no back-to-top, and no link to #scorecard-sec (line 477) anywhere. A reader who jumps from the scorecard to finding 7 has only a long manual scroll back; the footer ends on a single attribution sentence (renderFooter, lines 1032–1052).
recommendation: End the page with a quiet mono wayfinding row above the colophon: '↑ Back to top · Findings, by principle' — Fira Code, uppercase, tx-2, accent on hover. '#top' is a spec-defined fragment that scrolls to document top with no matching id needed; #scorecard-sec already exists. Preferred over a floating ↑ button (extra fixed chrome, off-brand) — the footer pair is the restrained shippable fix.
evidence: before-footer-dark.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 477, 1032–1052; grep for '#top'/'scrollTo' returns nothing
reasons: taste: Y — A very long flat flow with no route back up is a real dead-end, and the footer pair is the restrained answer — correctly rejecting the floating button that would have been off-brand; coordinate placement with C51 (nav above credit, colophon below).
risk: Y — '#top' is spec-defined with no id required, #scorecard-sec exists at line 477, and the additive footer row touches no invariant.
value: Y — A 12,000px document with provably no route back up is a genuine wayfinding gap, and the footer pair is the restrained fix versus floating chrome.

title: Scrollable code regions are unreachable by keyboard — add tabindex/role when a pre overflows
problem: figure.code pre scrolls both axes (overflow-x:auto, max-height:24rem, line 341) but the JS creates it with no tabindex (grep confirms zero in the file), so keyboard users cannot scroll it and the cut-off tokens are unreadable for them. Chrome/Firefox accessibility audits flag exactly this ('scrollable region not focusable'); the page is otherwise careful about a11y (skip-link, visually-hidden), so the gap is out of character.
recommendation: After render, if the pre actually overflows, set tabindex=0, role=region, and an aria-label from the figcaption location. The existing global :focus-visible accent outline (line 100) styles it for free. Hook into the same rAF callback as C04's fade sync.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 100, 341, 956–960; grep 'tabindex' returns nothing; overflow visible in before-code-citations-dark.png
reasons: taste: Y — The page is otherwise scrupulous about a11y (skip link, visually-hidden), so keyboard-unreachable scrollable evidence is out of character; the fix is invisible and rides C04's rAF hook.
risk: Y — Standard fix for a real audit-flagged a11y gap; the overflow check should also re-run on document.fonts.ready (piggyback C04's syncFade) since CDN fonts change scrollWidth.

value: Y — Keyboard users literally cannot read truncated evidence in 8 scrollable panes, and the page's existing a11y care (skip-link, focus styles) makes this gap look like an oversight audits will flag.

title: Footer links wrongly carry data-href-shown, so print drops the tool's provenance URLs
problem: The print rule appends hrefs only for links NOT marked data-href-shown (line 450) — the marker means 'the link text already shows the URL'. The masthead repo link is correctly marked (line 691; its text IS the URL), but both footer anchors also set it (lines 1042, 1046) despite their text being 'Magnificent Humanity Audit agent skill' and 'Cache Atelier' — inverting the attribute's documented meaning. A printed page loses the GitHub releases URL and cacheatelier.work entirely, exactly the links a paper reader cannot click.
recommendation: Delete the two setAttribute("data-href-shown","") calls on the footer anchors so the existing print rule appends their URLs.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 449–450 (rule + comment), 691 (correct usage), 1042, 1046 (inverted usage); before-footer-dark.png
reasons: taste: Y — The attribute's documented meaning is inverted on exactly the links a paper reader cannot click; deleting two lines restores the print machinery's intent (complementary with C44's correct use of the same mechanism).
risk: Y — Deleting two setAttribute calls restores the print rule's documented semantics with zero side effects; complementary with C44, not conflicting.
value: Y — Verified at lines 1042/1046: the attribute's meaning is inverted, so printed copies lose the only provenance URLs a paper reader cannot click — a two-line deletion.

title: Theme toggle semantics are self-contradicting: aria-pressed fights the action label; dead under noscript; D shortcut undocumented
problem: The visible label names the ACTION ('Light' while dark) but aria-pressed tracks the CURRENT theme (lines 464, 471, 1058), so a screen reader announces 'Light, toggle button, pressed' in dark mode — asserting light is on when it is off. With JS off, the hardcoded toggle still renders but does nothing. And the global D keyboard shortcut (lines 1061–1066) is documented nowhere — no title, no aria-keyshortcuts (grep confirms) — undiscoverable for those who'd want it and startling for anyone who brushes the keyboard.
recommendation: Treat it as an action button: drop aria-pressed, set a self-describing aria-label that updates with the text, attach title + aria-keyshortcuts to document the shortcut where the action lives, and hide the control under noscript. Visuals unchanged (the tap-target bump rides with C06).
css_js_sketch:
<noscript><style>#theme-toggle{display:none}</style></noscript>
/* in set(t) (line 1058) and the pre-paint script (line 464): */
btn.removeAttribute("aria-pressed");
var next = t==="dark" ? "light" : "dark";
btn.textContent = next==="light" ? "Light" : "Dark";
btn.setAttribute("aria-label","Switch to "+next+" theme");
/* once, in wireTheme(): */
btn.title="Toggle theme — or press D";
btn.setAttribute("aria-keyshortcuts","d");
<!-- markup (line 471): <button id="theme-toggle" type="button" aria-label="Switch to light theme">Light</button> -->
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 455–466, 471, 1054–1066; grep 'aria-keyshortcuts' returns nothing
amendments: Place the noscript style block in <head> (spec-clean location for noscript>style) rather than body, and add a .catch-free guard is not needed; rest as sketched.
reasons: taste: Y — A toggle announcing 'pressed' for the theme that is off is a genuine semantic contradiction; action-button semantics, noscript hiding, and documenting the D shortcut are all invisible correctness (layout side rides C06).
risk: Y — Action-button semantics are correct and both write sites (pre-paint script and set()) are covered; only the noscript placement needs the tidier variant.
value: Y — A screen reader announcing 'Light, pressed' while dark is active is factually wrong state communication, and a visible-but-dead control under noscript erodes trust; visuals ride with C06.

title: Put the system name first in document.title, guard the dangling dash, and drop lang="la" from the <title>
problem: init() sets document.title="Magnifica Humanitas Audit — "+(meta.system_name||"") (line 1086): every report shares the same long prefix so crowded tabs all truncate to the identical 'Magnifica Hum…' with the one distinguishing fact cut off, and a missing system_name leaves a dangling ' — '. The static <title> also carries lang="la" (line 8), which after the JS rewrite mislabels the English system name as Latin for screen readers.
recommendation: Order most-specific-first (system — brand), guard the empty case, and remove lang from the title element (the in-page <em lang="la"> elements are correctly scoped and stay).
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 8, 672, 967, 1086
reasons: taste: Y — Most-specific-first titles, a guarded dash, and removing the now-wrong lang='la' are the kind of detail a Stripe-grade page gets right without anyone noticing.
risk: Y — Single-call-site title rewrite with an empty-name guard, and removing lang='la' from <title> fixes a real screen-reader mislabel after the JS rewrite.
value: Y — Developers live in crowded tab bars — every report truncating to the identical 'Magnifica Hum…' hides the one distinguishing fact, and the lang=la mislabel is a real a11y wrinkle.

title: No meta description or Open Graph tags — shared links unfurl bare
problem: The head (lines 3–11) has no <meta name="description">, no og:*, no twitter:card (grep confirms). Pasting the report URL into Slack, iMessage, X or LinkedIn — the natural way an audit circulates — yields no summary and no card; search snippets fall back to arbitrary body text. Crawlers don't run the JS, so the static copy must stand alone — which it can, since the framing is constant across reports.
recommendation: Add static description/og/twitter tags reusing the dek's own sentence so the card speaks in the report's voice; optionally have init() sync og:title alongside document.title for the rare JS-executing scraper.
css_js_sketch:
<meta name="description" content="An anthropological audit reading a software system against the seven principles of Catholic social doctrine set out in the encyclical Magnifica Humanitas.">
<meta property="og:type" content="article">
<meta property="og:title" content="Magnifica Humanitas Audit">
<meta property="og:description" content="This reads the system against the seven principles of Catholic social doctrine, asking what its workings imply about the human person.">
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary">
/* in init(): */
var ogt=document.querySelector('meta[property="og:title"]');
if(ogt) ogt.setAttribute('content', document.title);
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 3–11; grep 'description'/'og:' returns nothing
amendments: Static tags only — drop the init() og:title sync, since scrapers don't run JS and the extra machinery buys nothing.
reasons: taste: Y — An audit circulates by being pasted into Slack and issues, and the static framing copy genuinely stands alone; the card should speak in the dek's own voice.
risk: Y — Static head tags with constant framing copy survive the no-JS crawler path by design; zero interaction with the data contract.
value: Y — Slack/iMessage/X unfurls are precisely how an audit circulates, and a bare card at share time is a first-impression failure the static dek text can fix for free.

title: Add a data-URI ✴ SVG favicon
problem: No <link rel="icon"> exists (grep confirms), so every tab shows the generic document glyph and file:// or hosted copies 404 on /favicon.ico. For a flagship artifact whose identity is built on the ✴ glyph set, the tab is the one place the brand never appears — and developers keep many tabs open.
recommendation: One self-contained data-URI SVG: a Flexoki black rounded tile with the ✴ glyph in paper #FFFCF0 (reads on both light and dark tab bars; system serif renders the glyph, no font dependency). Percent-encode the glyph and append U+FE0E so text presentation applies. Zero external requests; keeps the single-file constraint.
css_js_sketch: <link rel="icon" href='data:image/svg+xml,<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 100 100"><rect width="100" height="100" rx="16" fill="%23100F0F"/><text x="50" y="74" font-size="66" text-anchor="middle" fill="%23FFFCF0">%E2%9C%B4%EF%B8%8E</text></svg>'>
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 3–11; grep 'rel="icon' returns nothing
amendments: Percent-encode the spaces in the SVG data URI for strict URL validity; Safari ignoring SVG favicons degrades gracefully to the current state.
reasons: taste: Y — The ✴ is the brand and the tab is the one place it never appears; a data-URI SVG keeps the single-file constraint and adds zero requests.
risk: Y — Explicitly within the data-URI favicon allowance, zero external requests, and the quoting (single-quoted href, %23, U+FE0E) is correct.
value: Y — The tab is the one surface where the ✴ identity never appears, and the data-URI keeps the single-file constraint at zero cost.

title: Mobile rail: the confidence number stacks on its own line inside the inline meta strip
problem: Desktop sets .f-rail .f-conf b{display:block} (line 315) so the number sits under its label in the vertical rail; the ≤680px block (lines 431–432) flips the rail to a horizontal wrapped row but never overrides the block display, so the inline strip fractures — measured at 375px the conf box is 37px tall with 'confidence' and '88' stacked, inside what should be one quiet inline run before the title.
recommendation: In the mobile media query, render the value inline after its label.
evidence: /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-frail.png; measured conf box h=37 with b at y+17; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 314–315 vs 414–436
reasons: taste: Y — A 37px-tall stacked fragment inside what should be one quiet inline strip is visibly unfinished on mobile; one declaration fixes it.
risk: Y — One declaration inside the existing 680px block; no desktop or print exposure.
value: Y — A 37px-tall stacked fragment inside what should be one quiet inline strip is visible breakage on every mobile finding, fixed by one declaration.

title: Mobile masthead meta loses label–value pairing: gap within a pair equals gap between pairs
problem: At ≤680px .mast-meta{grid-template-columns:1fr; gap:.15rem 0} (line 427) stacks labels and values with one uniform ~2.4px gap — 'evidence' sits as close to the preceding scope text as to its own value 'Source code'. The block reads as one undifferentiated list; Gestalt pairing needs more space between pairs than within them.
recommendation: Zero the uniform gap and give each label (except the first) a top margin so pairs cluster.
evidence: /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-meta.png; measured label→own-value 2.4px and prior-value→label 2.4px; final-rail-stars-v3.html line 427
reasons: taste: Y — Gestalt pairing — more space between pairs than within them — is exactly the felt-not-noticed polish standard; the current uniform 2.4px gap dissolves the label-value structure.
risk: Y — Scoped to the existing mobile query and :first-child correctly targets the first label in the alternating grid.
value: Y — With identical 2.4px gaps within and between pairs, mobile readers cannot tell which value belongs to which label in the first screen they see.

title: Mobile scorecard strands '01 ●' on its own line above the principle name
problem: At ≤680px .sc-row{grid-template-columns:1fr; row-gap:.4rem} (line 417) puts the ordinal+dot marker on its own row above the name (measured: marker row y=1814.8 h=17.3, name at y=1838.5). Each principle opens with a tiny orphaned number-and-dot line that reads as debris rather than the row's ordinal.
recommendation: Collapse .sc-row to a wrapped flex row on mobile so the marker sits inline before the name, letting the chip/count wrap naturally below.
evidence: /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-scorecard.png ('03 ●' alone above THE UNIVERSAL DESTINATION OF GOODS); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 417, 421–423
reasons: taste: Y — '03 ●' stranded as debris above each principle name is visibly wrong on mobile; the flex collapse is minimal — coordinate with C01's desktop nowrap and 680px wrap rules so .sc-row is rewritten once.
risk: Y — The flex collapse verifies at 375px (16rem basis fits beside the marker) and is confined to the 680px block, leaving desktop grid untouched.
value: Y — '03 ●' stranded alone above each principle name reads as debris at the top of every mobile scorecard row.

title: Give 'Dropped' and 'Limits' the one-line leads the first closing has, and use the full 'Limits of this audit' caption
problem: The coda is asymmetric: 'What the system does well' opens with a considered lead sentence (lines 1092–1093), but 'Dropped and consolidated' and 'Limits' pass null leads (lines 1101, 1116) and drop straight into terse bullets — the 'appendix dump' feel. The third caption is just 'Limits' while its own section aria-label is 'Limits of this audit' (line 481), breaking parallelism with the two full-phrase captions above it.
recommendation: Pass a one-sentence lead to both remaining movements in the same voice as the first, and title the third 'Limits of this audit' to match its landmark label.
evidence: before-closings-dark.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 481, 1092–1093, 1101, 1116
amendments: Keep each lead to the single sentence proposed — no longer — so the closings stay terse end-matter.
reasons: taste: Y — The asymmetric coda — one considered lead then two bare dumps — and the caption that breaks parallelism with its own aria-label are real register failures; the proposed copy stays in voice.
risk: Y — Copy-only change through the existing renderList lead parameter, and it restores parallelism with the aria-label at line 481.
value: Y — The asymmetric coda and the caption/aria-label mismatch are real but editorial — leads improve finish without being load-bearing for trust.

title: Dropped-list mono metadata wraps mid-pair ('conf 35 · reason / charitable reading')
problem: The renderer emits label and value as separate inline nodes with no wrap control (lines 1105–1113), so lines break between a label and its value: before-closings-dark.png shows 'conf 35 · reason' ending a line with 'charitable reading' orphaned below, '· reason' / 'below threshold', and 'reason merged' / 'into F1' — three of four items break awkwardly.
recommendation: Wrap each label+value pair in a white-space:nowrap span so wrapping happens only at the ' — ' and ' · ' separators; the pairs are short, so nowrap cannot overflow the column. (The merged-into pair becomes a link per C08.)
evidence: before-closings-dark.png (items 1, 3, 4 of Dropped); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 392–393, 1105–1113
amendments: Build on C08's linked merged-into pair so the dropped-list builder is rewritten once.
reasons: taste: Y — Three of four dropped items breaking between a label and its value reads as carelessness in the section about editorial discipline; nowrap pairs is the same fix grammar as C02 (coordinate the merged-into pair with C08's link).

risk: Y — Short nowrap pairs cannot overflow the column, but this rewrites the same dropped-builder nodes as C08's merged-into link — apply both as one coordinated edit with the anchor inside the reason pair.
value: Y — Verified in before-closings-dark.png: three of four dropped items break between label and value, making the metadata genuinely harder to parse.

title: Define an on-brand ::selection instead of the OS default blue
problem: No ::selection rule exists anywhere (grep confirms). Selecting text — constant behavior for readers of a quotable report — paints the stock saturated UA blue across the warm paper and the #100F0F ground: the one interaction color that ignores the tuned Flexoki accents.
recommendation: One rule using the color-mix pattern already used for chip tints (lines 45–47, 77–79) so the wash derives from the theme's own accent and flips automatically with data-theme.
css_js_sketch: ::selection{background:color-mix(in oklab, var(--accent) 28%, var(--bg)); color:var(--tx)}
evidence: grep '::selection' returns nothing; color-mix precedent at final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 45–47, 77–79
reasons: taste: Y — Stock UA blue across warm paper is the one interaction color outside the tuned palette; deriving the wash from the theme's own accent via the existing color-mix pattern is on-brand and invisible until touched.
risk: Y — One rule using the file's established color-mix pattern; computed selection contrast verifies comfortably in both themes (≈8.3:1 dark, ≈12:1 light).
value: Y — Readers select and copy this report constantly and stock UA blue ignores the tuned palette — a one-line nicety worth bundling.

title: Masthead and footer links have no hover state; existing hovers snap instead of easing
problem: The repo link (.mm-value a, line 214) and both footer links (line 400) have no :hover rule at all — the only link hovers are the scorecard rows and the toggle (lines 185, 276–278) — and none of the hovers that do exist are eased, while the body's theme change is eased at .2s (line 95). The artifact's only outbound credits give zero interaction feedback.
recommendation: One shared quiet hover (text shifts to primary ink, underline keeps the accent — matching the toggle's hover logic) plus a single .15s ease on links and the toggle. The reduced-motion block (line 410) already neutralizes it.
css_js_sketch:
a,#theme-toggle{transition:color .15s ease, text-decoration-color .15s ease}
.mm-value a:hover,footer.report a:hover{color:var(--tx); text-decoration-color:var(--accent)}
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 95, 185, 214, 276–278, 400, 410; before-masthead-dark.png, before-footer-dark.png
reasons: taste: Y — The artifact's only outbound credits giving zero hover feedback, and every hover snapping while the theme eases, is a small coherence gap; one shared eased hover closes it without new vocabulary.
risk: Y — Global a transition is safe (reduced-motion block neutralizes it) and hover states reuse existing tokens only.
value: Y — Zero hover feedback on the only outbound credits is unpolished but minor; one shared rule fixes it.

title: Apply dedash() to scorecard titles so the same title reads identically in both places
problem: The finding card runs titles through dedash() so em-dashes become spaced en-dashes (line 935, the settled mono-title style), but the scorecard cross-reference uses curl() only (line 825) — the same sentence reads '…engagement — including dwell —…' in the scorecard and '…engagement – including dwell –…' on the card. A reader navigating scorecard→card sees the punctuation silently change.
recommendation: Apply dedash() in the scorecard too, making the title one canonical string everywhere (the en-dash also wraps better in the narrow bullet column).
css_js_sketch:
/* renderScorecard, line 825 */
a.appendChild(el("span","scf-title", dedash(curl(f.title||"Untitled finding"))));
evidence: before-scorecard-top-dark.png vs before-finding-card-dark.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 558, 825, 935
reasons: taste: Y — The same title silently changing punctuation between scorecard and card breaks the reader's trust in precision; one canonical dedash'd string is a one-line fix.
risk: Y — One-line reuse of the existing dedash() helper at a single call site; makes titles canonical across both surfaces.
value: Y — Punctuation silently changing between the scorecard and the card it links to is a subtle consistency leak; one-word fix.

title: Format the masthead date for reading inside a <time> element
problem: The date row prints the raw ISO string '2026-06-02' (line 679) in serif with global oldstyle figures (onum, line 94) — the 6s ascend while 0/2 sit at x-height, a bouncy machine-format string in an otherwise literary masthead, and it carries no <time datetime> semantics. (Lining figures were proposed as the minimal fix, but a prose date is where oldstyle figures are actually correct.)
recommendation: Render 'June 2, 2026' inside <time datetime="2026-06-02">, falling back to the raw string when it does not parse — fixes the figure style and the semantics in one move.
evidence: before-masthead-dark.png / before-masthead-light.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 94, 679, 699–701
reasons: taste: Y — A raw ISO string in an otherwise literary masthead is the first thing a careful reader notices; 'June 2, 2026' in a <time> element is more itself in both register and semantics — and C51's colophon should reuse the same formatter.
risk: Y — Regex-based formatting avoids Date timezone pitfalls entirely and falls back to the raw string; purely additive in the rows loop.
value: Y — '2026-06-02' raw in a literary masthead is slightly machine-flavored, but ISO dates don't actively hurt a technical reader; the <time> semantics are a bonus.

title: Keep hyphenated identifiers like 'home-mixer' from fracturing in the scope row
problem: The scope value wraps mid-identifier in both theme shots — '(home-' ends one line and 'mixer, phoenix, grok…' opens the next — momentarily reading as the word 'home'. The value is a plain text node (line 700) and .mm-value's overflow-wrap:anywhere + word-break:break-word (line 213) makes it maximally willing to break.
recommendation: When rendering meta values, wrap any token containing an intra-word hyphen in a nowrap span so system identifiers stay whole; ordinary prose is untouched (safer than U+2011, which risks a fallback-glyph mismatch in EB Garamond).
evidence: before-masthead-dark.png / before-masthead-light.png ('home-' / 'mixer' break); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 207–213, 699–701
reasons: taste: Y — 'home-' / 'mixer' momentarily reading as the word 'home' is a real comprehension stutter; protecting hyphenated identifiers with nowrap spans is safer than U+2011 and invisible elsewhere.

risk: Y — Safer than U+2011 as argued; only residual edge is a pathologically long hyphenated token refusing to break, which mm-value's anywhere fallback no longer catches inside the span — acceptable for identifier-length tokens.
value: Y — Verified in the masthead: 'home-' / 'mixer' fractures a system identifier in the first viewport, momentarily misreading as the word 'home' for the audience that knows these repos.

title: Display the repository URL without its scheme; let print append the full address
problem: The repository row prints the raw 'https://github.com/…' verbatim, underlined end to end (renderHeader lines 689–698). The scheme is noise in an editorial masthead; Stripe-grade documents display 'github.com/…' and keep the full URL in href. The print machinery already handles text-vs-href divergence via data-href-shown (line 450), so dropping that attribute makes print correctly append the full URL.
recommendation: Strip the scheme from the displayed text (href stays complete), keep the existing <wbr> insertion after slashes, and remove data-href-shown on this anchor.
evidence: before-masthead-dark.png / before-masthead-light.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 450, 689–698
reasons: taste: Y — Dropping the scheme is the established editorial convention for displayed URLs, and it correctly uses the print machinery C29 repairs — display loses noise, print gains the full address.
risk: Y — href stays complete, the <wbr> pattern is preserved, and dropping data-href-shown correctly re-engages the existing print URL rule.
value: Y — Scheme-stripping is an editorial nicety, and it correctly reuses the existing print machinery — note it interacts benignly with C29's attribute cleanup.

title: Fix the collapsed margin under 'From Magnifica Humanitas' so the cap binds to its quotes
problem: The cap is authored to sit .2rem above its first quote (.cite-cap margin:1.4rem 0 .2rem, line 350), but the adjacent .cite's 1rem top margin (line 356) collapses with it to a flat 1rem — the authored tight grouping never renders, so the label floats equidistant between the prose above and the quote it introduces (gap equals the citation-to-citation gap). Line 357's '.cite + .cite{margin-top:1rem}' is fully redundant with 356 — evidence of the confused intent.
recommendation: Move inter-citation spacing onto the sibling selector only and bind the cap to the first citation with a small explicit gap.
evidence: before-code-citations-dark.png (cap↔quote gap equals quote↔quote gap); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 350, 356–357
reasons: taste: Y — Margin collapse silently defeating the authored cap-to-quote binding is a real bug in intent; the sibling-selector rewrite restores what the designer already meant (sequence before C47's tokenization).
risk: Y — Correct margin-collapse diagnosis; every first .cite is provably preceded by a .cite-cap (lines 964–984) so the sibling selectors cover all cases.
value: Y — The collapsed margin defeating the authored cap-to-quote binding is real but reads as ordinary spacing to non-designers; coordinate with C47's tokens.

title: Add .cite-quote to the text-wrap:pretty list
problem: Line 137 grants text-wrap:pretty to glosses, structural notes, remedies and explanations — but not .cite-quote, the most precious typography in the card. The §170 quote already shows the consequence: a long first line breaking to a short second with no rag protection against future orphans.
recommendation: Add .cite-quote to the existing pretty list (consistent with its siblings; balance would be the bolder pull-quote treatment, pretty matches the page's restraint).
css_js_sketch:
/* line 137 */
.f-explain p,.cite-quote,.cite-gloss,.dek,.summary p,.sc-note,.structural p,ul.remedy li,ul.plain li{text-wrap:pretty}
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 137, 360; before-finding-card-light.png (§170 quote rag)
reasons: taste: Y — The most precious typography on the card being the one block without rag protection is an obvious omission from the existing pretty list; one selector.
risk: Y — One selector added to an existing list; no interaction with C20's text-indent.
value: Y — One selector added to an existing list, protecting the rag of the page's most precious text — costless.

title: Tokenize the drifting caption gaps, intra-finding block margins, and asterism margins
problem: Spacing values playing the same role drift by small unjustified amounts: caption bottom margins are 1rem (.sum-cap, 233), 1.2rem (.sc-cap, 247) and 1.3rem (.m-cap, 380); intra-finding block top margins are 1.1rem (figure.code, 331), 1.2rem (remedy/structural, 370/375) and 1.4rem (cite-cap, 350); the asterism is 2.8rem top / 2.6rem bottom (296). None of these deltas reads as hierarchy — they read as drift.
recommendation: Tokenize the rhythm: one caption gap, one intra-finding block gap, a symmetric asterism margin. Coordinate with C12 (sum-cap), C13 (sc-cap), C22 (remedy gap) and C45 (cite-cap) which set the deliberate exceptions.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 233, 247, 296, 331, 350, 370, 375, 380; before-synthesis-dark.png vs before-closings-dark.png
amendments: Apply last, after C12/C13/C22/C45 establish the deliberate exceptions, so the tokens encode final intent rather than the current drift.
Drop the .sc-cap line from this candidate and let C13's contextual .9rem win — they set conflicting bottom margins on the same property.
Apply last, after C12/C13/C22/C45 set their deliberate values, or the token pass will overwrite them.
reasons: taste: Y — The drifting near-values read as cascade noise, not hierarchy, and tokenizing them is how this stylesheet already works.
risk: Y — Tokenization itself is safe, but it CONFLICTS with C13 on .sc-cap margin-bottom (1.2rem vs .9rem) and must defer to the candidates it acknowledges as deliberate exceptions (C12, C22, C45).
value: Y — Pure system hygiene no reader will perceive, acceptable only as the cleanup pass bundled behind the accepted spacing fixes — mild sequencing conflict with C12/C13/C22/C45.

title: Set the closing captions in the running caption voice, not the masthead overline's loud tracking
problem: The three closing captions (.m-cap, line 380) are .86rem with the loud --track-label (.13em) — the masthead overline's frame voice (lines 189–192) — while sibling section captions .sum-cap/.sc-cap are .92rem with --track-cap (.06em). The comment at line 30 shows a deliberate bookend, but three consecutive loud-tracked section heads read as a different register from the two captions above them; one closing element could bookend the overline, three section heads cannot.
recommendation: Set .m-cap to the running caption voice (.92rem, --track-cap), reserving --track-label for the masthead overline alone.
css_js_sketch: .movement .m-cap{font-variant:small-caps; letter-spacing:var(--track-cap); font-size:.92rem; color:var(--tx-2); margin:0 0 1.2rem; font-weight:500}
evidence: before-closings-dark.png vs before-scorecard-top-dark.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 30, 189–192, 231–234, 245–248, 380
reasons: taste: Y — A bookend executed three times in a row stops being a bookend — sections at the same outline level as Synthesis should share its caption voice, and the loud --track-label is rightly reserved for the overline alone.
risk: Y — Single-rule retune using existing tracking tokens; no layout consequence.
value: Y — The register shift in the closing captions is perceptible only on direct comparison with the captions far above; quiet improvement, not reader-blocking.

title: Give the masthead more air above the overline
problem: header.report has padding:3.6rem 0 0 (line 188), putting the overline ≈68px from the viewport top — visibly less than the masthead's own internal bands below the dek (1.6rem meta + 2.4rem rule + 2.4rem section). The formal serif masthead starts slightly crowded against browser chrome rather than opening like a title page.
