recommendation: Increase desktop top padding to ~5.5rem so the document opens with deliberate white space; keep 3.6rem under the 680px breakpoint where vertical economy matters.
css_js_sketch:
header.report{padding:5.5rem 0 0}
@media(max-width:680px){ header.report{padding:3.6rem 0 0} }
evidence: before-masthead-dark.png / before-masthead-light.png; final-rail-stars-v3.html line 188
reasons: taste: Y — A title page should open with deliberate white space, and the current top padding is visibly tighter than the masthead's own internal bands; keeping 3.6rem on mobile shows the right judgment.
risk: Y — Padding-only change with the mobile value preserved; compatible with C06's toggle placement at top:1.5rem.
value: Y — More opening air is a taste call that does improve the title-page feel, but nobody distrusts the current 68px.

title: Support inline code in closing bullets so Rust paths stop rendering in serif
problem: The first Limits bullet sets util::phoenix_request::build_prediction_request in EB Garamond (before-footer-dark.png) because setProse is text-only (line 560). Everywhere else the report is scrupulous about code-in-Fira-Code; a long Rust path in serif with serif underscores is the one place that discipline lapses.
recommendation: Teach setProse to split on backtick spans and emit <code> for them (data then carries the identifier in backticks), with a small inline-code size so mono sits on the serif line.
evidence: before-footer-dark.png / before-closings-dark.png (Limits, first bullet); final-rail-stars-v3.html line 560
amendments: Document the backtick convention in the data schema so authors know the contract, and confirm no existing prose fields carry literal backticks.
Split on backticks BEFORE applying curl() — the sketch curls the whole string first, so apostrophes and quotes inside code spans would be typographically mangled; curl only the non-code segments.
Requires the data/template side to emit backticks around identifiers — confirm the skill's findings schema can carry them.
reasons: taste: Y — A Rust path in serif with serif underscores is the one lapse in the page's code-in-mono discipline; the backtick mini-syntax is acceptable because the skill controls the data contract.
risk: Y — setProse is used on every prose path so the ordering bug in the sketch would corrupt code identifiers; with the amendment it is safe and unmatched backticks already pass through literally.
value: Y — A long Rust path in serif with serif underscores, verified in the footer shot, is the one visible lapse in the report's otherwise scrupulous code discipline.

title: Add a bare 'copy' text-button to the code figure caption
problem: The figure header carries path and language but no way to copy the snippet — the one action a developer reader actually takes with evidence (to grep, file an issue, test a remedy). Selecting inside a horizontally scrolling pre is fiddly. Done as a bare mono text-button it adds zero visual weight.
recommendation: Insert a 'copy' text button between loc and lang: Fira Code at caption size, tx-2 ink, no border or fill, 'copied' feedback for ~1.2s, hidden in print. navigator.clipboard is available in file:// and https contexts; keep the button only when the API exists.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 332, 950–961 (figcaption contains only .loc and .lang)
amendments: The figcaption is justify-content:space-between, so a third child centers mid-bar — give .code-copy{margin-left:auto} (with lang keeping a fixed gap) or wrap copy+lang in a right group; also add a .catch() so a clipboard permission rejection doesn't throw unhandled.
reasons: taste: Y — Borderline gadgetry, but copying evidence is the one action this audience actually takes and the bare lowercase mono text-button passes the Stripe test — keep it exactly this quiet, no icon, no border, ever.
risk: Y — Feature-detected, print-hidden, and dependency-free, but the flex placement in the sketch visibly mislands and needs the amendment.

value: Y — Copying a snippet to grep or file an issue is the single action a developer takes with evidence, selecting inside a scrolling pre is genuinely fiddly, and the bare-text treatment matches Stripe-grade docs.

title: Disable Fira Code ligatures inside code evidence so snippets are verbatim
problem: body enables liga globally (line 94) and Fira Code ships calt programming ligatures on by default, so evidence snippets render '::', '->', '=>' as merged glyphs rather than the literal source characters (the re-spaced 'Self::apply' is visible in both theme shots). For forensic evidence quoted at file:line, glyphs should be character-faithful.
recommendation: Turn off ligatures and contextual alternates inside figure.code only; titles and labels elsewhere keep the default.
css_js_sketch: figure.code code{font-variant-ligatures:none; font-feature-settings:"liga" 0, "calt" 0}
evidence: before-code-citations-dark.png / before-finding-card-light.png ('Self::apply'); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 94, 98, 344
reasons: taste: Y — For evidence quoted at file:line, character-faithful glyphs are more trustworthy than Fira Code's merged ligatures, and scoping the change to figure.code preserves the mono voice everywhere else.
risk: Y — One scoped CSS rule; improves evidence fidelity with zero interaction with the highlighter's markup.
value: Y — Most developers read ligatured code daily without doubting fidelity — verbatim glyphs are the purist's choice and the rule is one line scoped to evidence.

title: Theme the Firefox scrollbar with scrollbar-color to match the WebKit thumb
problem: Code scrollbars are themed only for WebKit (lines 342–343); Firefox gets scrollbar-width:thin (line 341) with UA default colors (grep confirms no scrollbar-color), which — especially before C10 lands — can render a light default thumb inside the dark code panel.
recommendation: Add the standard two-value scrollbar-color alongside the existing rules, using the same tokens so both engines render an identically muted, theme-following thumb.
css_js_sketch: figure.code pre{scrollbar-width:thin; scrollbar-color:var(--ui-3) var(--code-bg)}
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 341–343; grep 'scrollbar-color' returns nothing
reasons: taste: Y — Theming one engine's scrollbar and not the other's is a parity gap with a one-line standard fix using the same tokens.
risk: Y — Standard-property one-liner using existing tokens; complements C10 rather than depending on it.
value: Y — Firefox-only thumb color parity is a one-line guard that matters more before C10 lands than after.

title: Unify the three near-identical mono micro-sizes on one token
problem: Within one card the mono voice uses .70rem (figcaption, 332), .72rem (pre, 341; .dc, 392) and .74rem (cite-ref, 358; skip-link, 101) — differences of ~0.4px at the 16px root that read as cascade noise, not hierarchy, and make future tuning error-prone.
recommendation: Define one --mono-micro token (.72rem) for the figcaption, pre, cite-ref and dropped-counts; if a deliberate step is wanted later, change it in one place.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 101, 332, 341, 358, 392
reasons: taste: Y — Three mono sizes 0.4px apart are cascade noise that makes future tuning error-prone; one --mono-micro token is pure housekeeping (land with C19's prose tokens).
risk: Y — Sub-pixel size unification with no measure consequences (cite-ref's 3.5rem column absorbs the .02rem change); skip-link intentionally left out is fine.
value: Y — 0.4px deltas are invisible to every reader; worth taking only as bundled token hygiene.

title: Empty-principle state: accessible count and a non-list note
problem: When a principle has zero engaging findings the count cell renders a bare '—' (line 810) — screen readers announce 'em dash' where siblings say 'N findings' — and the note is wrapped as ul > li > p (lines 839–847), announcing 'list, 1 item' for a single sentence. Not visible in this dataset (all seven principles engage), but the template ships the state and an honors-heavy audit will surface it.
recommendation: Keep the em dash visually but pair it with visually-hidden 'no findings' text; render the note as a grid-aligned div + p instead of a one-item list.
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 286–287, 810, 839–847
amendments: Also update the two mobile selectors at lines 418–420 (ul.sc-findings, ul.sc-findings > li) when loosening to .sc-findings, or the empty-state div loses its single-column collapse.
reasons: taste: Y — The template ships the empty-principle state even if this dataset doesn't exercise it; a screen reader announcing 'em dash' and a one-item list for a sentence are real flaws an honors-heavy audit will surface.
risk: Y — Sound a11y fix for a shipped-but-latent state; the selector loosening just needs to cover the 680px block too.
value: Y — Not reachable in this dataset, but the template ships the state and the next honors-heavy audit will surface the bare 'em dash' announcement.

title: Hide the empty-rail ghost rows at the 680px collapse
problem: Eight sections emit an empty el("div","rail") as the grid's first child (JS lines 664, 732, 750, 761, 852, 892, 1013, 1037). At ≤680px, .grid{grid-template-columns:1fr; row-gap:.5rem} (line 416) turns each into a 0-height first row plus a .5rem gap — a uniform 8px dead offset above every section, closing rule and ref-less citation cell (line 976).
recommendation: Hide empty marginalia cells when the grid collapses; the DOM-built divs are truly empty so :empty matches.
evidence: Measured at 375px: mast grid top 101.8, rail h=0, content-col top 109.8; final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 416, 664, 732, 750, 761, 852, 892, 976, 1013, 1037
reasons: taste: Y — A uniform 8px dead offset above every mobile section is exactly the kind of invisible slack a finished product doesn't carry; :empty on the DOM-built rails is the minimal fix.
risk: Y — :empty provably matches — el() creates the rail divs with no child nodes at all — and the rule is scoped inside the existing mobile query.
value: Y — A uniform 8px dead offset on mobile reads as spacing, not breakage, but the :empty rule is a free two-line cleanup.

title: Mobile rail: let the ✴ share the ENGAGES line instead of sitting alone
problem: In the ≤680px rail row, .f-engages is a single flex item whose max-content width forces it to wrap below the star, so the ✴ opens the finding as a stray lone glyph line above a 2–3 line meta strip (measured star at y=4522 alone, ENGAGES label at y=4549.8) — four lines of marginalia before the title.
recommendation: Use display:contents on .f-engages in the mobile query so the label and items become flex items of .f-rail and flow inline after the star.
evidence: /tmp/mh-mobile/mob-frail.png (lone ✴ line above ENGAGES); final-rail-stars-v3.html lines 304–306, 431–432
reasons: taste: Y — A stray lone glyph line above the mobile meta strip is visible debris; display:contents on a div of styled spans is safe here since the element carries no semantics to flatten.

risk: Y — display:contents on semantics-free spans is safe in all current engines, but flag the interaction with C18: its hanging text-indent on fe-items must be reset (or desktop-scoped) in this mobile row or first lines shift left oddly.
value: Y — The lone ✴ line on mobile is mildly awkward rather than broken; display:contents is fine on these plain divs and costs one rule.

title: Add text-size-adjust to prevent iOS landscape font inflation
problem: html carries only scroll-behavior (line 90); with no text-size-adjust, iOS Safari in landscape arbitrarily inflates some blocks (prose, but not the mono rail labels), breaking the tuned 19px measure and the rail's type hierarchy.
recommendation: One declaration on html alongside the existing scroll-behavior.
css_js_sketch: html{scroll-behavior:smooth; -webkit-text-size-adjust:100%; text-size-adjust:100%}
evidence: final-rail-stars-v3.html line 90; grep 'text-size-adjust' returns nothing
reasons: taste: Y — One zero-cost standard declaration protects the tuned 19px measure and the rail hierarchy from iOS landscape inflation; every Stripe-grade page ships it.
risk: Y — Inert everywhere except the iOS landscape case it guards; one declaration on the existing html rule.
value: Y — Standard guard against iOS landscape inflation that would visibly distort the tuned measure; one declaration, no downside.
